{"key_event":"Reza Shah’s authoritarian centralization","reasoning":"By the late 1930s, Iran had a far more capable central state: army, police, taxation, courts, schools, railways, trade controls, and provincial administration reached much deeper into society, though oil politics still showed external limits. Civil society fell sharply as tribal autonomy, clerical jurisdiction, bazaar independence, press, parties, unions, and elite notables were suppressed, with only limited offset from the emergence of a state-educated middle class.","society_change":-1.4,"society_power":2.8,"state_change":1.6,"state_power":7.8}
